subject: Company Law
lesson_title: Remedies for Breach of Directors' Duties

questions.question: A director of SolarGrid Ltd secretly profits from a contract she arranged between SolarGrid and a company she controls. The board discovers the breach. Which TWO primary remedies are available to SolarGrid against the director under S233 CA 2014?

options.A: Criminal prosecution under S248 CA 2014 and a mandatory disqualification order
options.B: An account of profits (restoring the secret profit to the company) AND/OR compensation for any loss suffered by the company from the breach
options.C: Rescission of the contract and an automatic restriction order under S819 CA 2014
options.D: A winding-up petition under S569 and a personal liability declaration under S610 CA 2014

questions.explanation: S233 CA 2014 provides two primary civil remedies for breach of directors' duties: (1) account of profits — the director must disgorge any profit made by reason of the breach; and (2) indemnification — the director must compensate the company for any loss it suffered. These remedies may be pursued cumulatively. The court has discretion in fashioning the appropriate remedy depending on whether the breach caused identifiable loss or merely unauthorised gain.

questions.question: The board of Precision Ltd discovers that a director diverted a corporate contract to his own company. The board wishes to ratify the breach by ordinary resolution so as to relieve the director of liability. Under Bamford v Bamford [1970] Ch 212, which condition is essential for ratification to be effective?

options.A: Ratification must be by special resolution (75%) — ordinary resolution is insufficient for breach of fiduciary duty
options.B: The ratifying members must be fully informed of all material facts; the ratification must be by a genuine majority with the conflicted director unable to use their own shares to manufacture a majority
options.C: Ratification is only effective if the company suffered no financial loss from the breach
options.D: Ratification must be confirmed by the court within 21 days of the general meeting

questions.explanation: Bamford v Bamford [1970] Ch 212 (EW CA) establishes that ratification of a director's breach of duty requires: (i) full disclosure of all material facts to the members; (ii) a genuine majority vote — the director whose breach is being ratified cannot use their own shareholding to produce the majority that relieves them of liability (this would be fraud on the minority). Where these conditions are met, an ordinary resolution suffices. Where a director uses their own votes to ratify their own wrong, the ratification may be void as a fraud on the minority.

questions.question: Directors of MarineCo Ltd diverted to themselves a lucrative shipbuilding contract that belonged to MarineCo, passed resolutions at a shareholders' meeting purporting to ratify the diversion, and used their own shares to ensure the resolution passed. A minority shareholder seeks to challenge this. Which authority establishes that ratification in these circumstances is invalid?

options.A: Foss v Harbottle [1843] — the company is the proper plaintiff; minority cannot challenge
options.B: Cook v Deeks [1916] 1 AC 554 (PC) — directors cannot ratify a fraud on the company by voting their own shares to approve their own wrongdoing
options.C: Aberdeen Railway v Blaikie [1854] — the no-conflict rule is strict
options.D: Peso Silver Mines v Cropper [1966] — bona fide refusal by the board extinguishes the claim

questions.explanation: Cook v Deeks [1916] 1 AC 554 (PC) is the leading authority establishing that a fraud on the company cannot be ratified by the wrongdoers using their own votes at a shareholders' meeting. In Cook v Deeks, directors diverted a contract from the company and then purported to pass a shareholders' resolution extinguishing the company's right to sue. The Privy Council held the resolution invalid — wrongdoers cannot ratify their own fraud by manufacturing a majority from their own shareholding.

questions.question: A director of BuildRight Ltd entered into a contract on behalf of the company with a third party, Construct plc. The director had an undisclosed conflict in relation to the contract. The company seeks to rescind the contract. Under S227(2) CA 2014, in what circumstances will the rights of Construct plc as a third party be protected?

options.A: Third parties are always protected — the company cannot rescind any contract on the ground of a director's breach of duty
options.B: Construct plc's rights are protected if it dealt with the company in good faith and did not have actual notice of the director's breach or conflict of interest
options.C: Third party protection applies only if the contract has been fully performed on both sides
options.D: Third party protection requires Construct plc to have obtained court confirmation of the transaction within 30 days of signing

questions.explanation: S227(2) CA 2014 provides that where a director breaches a duty to the company, a third party who dealt with the company in good faith (without actual notice of the breach) is not affected by the breach. This protects innocent third parties from having contracts rescinded due to director misfeasance of which they were unaware. Where a third party has knowledge of the director's breach, they cannot rely on S227(2) protection.

questions.question: HighRise Ltd holds 100% of the shares in LowRise Ltd. All shareholders of HighRise Ltd, which in turn is the sole shareholder of LowRise Ltd, are aware that the directors of LowRise have breached their duties. A general meeting of LowRise's sole shareholder (HighRise Ltd) ratifies the breach. Is this effective?

options.A: No — ratification always requires an independent third party to confirm it
options.B: Potentially yes — Bamford v Bamford confirms that a genuine informed majority may ratify a director's breach; where HighRise as 100% shareholder is fully informed and it is not a fraud on LowRise's minority (which has none), the ratification may be effective
options.C: No — subsidiaries cannot be bound by resolutions of parent companies
options.D: Yes — any unanimous written resolution of shareholders automatically extinguishes director liability regardless of disclosure

questions.explanation: Where there is a 100% shareholder fully informed of all material facts, ratification under Bamford v Bamford [1970] may be effective provided it is not a fraud on a minority (there is none in a 100% subsidiary) and does not infringe third party rights or statutory provisions. The Duomatic principle may also apply. However, if the breach involves fraud on creditors or is a statutory offence (e.g., S239 loans to directors), ratification cannot override the statutory prohibition.

questions.question: Maria, a director of CleanTech Ltd, was removed from the board under S146 CA 2014 by ordinary resolution after a dispute with co-shareholders. She claims she is entitled to compensation for the loss of her directorship. Under S146 CA 2014, which of the following is correct?

options.A: S146 provides that removal of a director automatically entitles them to compensation equivalent to 12 months' remuneration
options.B: S146 CA 2014 preserves any contractual rights Maria may have — if she had a service contract, she may claim compensation for its breach under general contract law, but S146 itself confers no automatic right to compensation
options.C: A removed director has a right to compensation only if the removal was effected without the required 28-day special notice
options.D: A removed director is entitled to compensation under S146(5) equivalent to the value of their shares at the date of removal

questions.explanation: S146 CA 2014 provides that a director may be removed by ordinary resolution with 28 days' special notice. S146(7) expressly preserves any rights to compensation or damages that the director may have under their service contract or otherwise. S146 itself creates no automatic right to compensation — the removed director must rely on any contractual entitlement (e.g., damages for wrongful dismissal under their service contract). The removal power under S146 cannot be excluded by the constitution.

questions.question: S146 CA 2014 requires that a director being removed be given special notice. What is the required period and what rights does the targeted director have?

options.A: 21-day special notice; the director has no right to speak at the meeting
options.B: 28-day special notice to the company (which must notify the director); the director has the right to make written representations to be circulated to members (S146(4)) and to speak at the meeting
options.C: 14-day special notice; the director may make oral representations only
options.D: 28-day special notice; the director's representations may only be circulated if they are less than 1,000 words

questions.explanation: S146(3) CA 2014 requires 28 days' special notice of a resolution to remove a director. Under S146(4), when such notice is received by the company, the director concerned has the right to have written representations (of a reasonable length) sent to every member to whom notice of the meeting is sent. The director also has the right to attend and speak at the meeting. These procedural rights cannot be waived by the company.

questions.question: Where a company discovers that a director made a secret profit of €200,000 but the company suffered no independently calculable financial loss, what remedy can the company pursue?

options.A: No remedy is available — without proven loss, there is nothing to recover
options.B: An account of profits — requiring the director to disgorge the full €200,000 profit to the company, regardless of whether the company suffered any loss
options.C: Compensation limited to nominal damages of €1 to mark the principle of the breach
options.D: A remedy is available only if the profit was made at the direct expense of the company

questions.explanation: The no-profit rule operates independently of loss: a director who makes a secret profit by reason of their fiduciary position must account for it whether or not the company suffered equivalent financial loss. This is confirmed by Regal Hastings v Gulliver [1942] and AIB v Diamond [2011]. The remedy of account of profits focuses on the defendant's gain rather than the plaintiff's loss — it is an equitable remedy designed to strip unjust enrichment from the fiduciary.

questions.question: A director of AllStar Ltd entered into a contract on behalf of the company with a supplier in which she had an undisclosed interest. The company wishes to set aside the contract. Under Irish company law, what type of transaction is this and what is the effect?

options.A: The contract is void ab initio — no action is required to set it aside
options.B: The contract is voidable at the company's election — the company may affirm or rescind it, provided third party rights have not intervened and rescission is still possible
options.C: The contract is automatically rescinded once the director's conflict is established
options.D: The contract remains fully binding — the director's conflict is irrelevant to third party contractual rights

questions.explanation: A contract entered into by a director in breach of their duty of disclosure and conflict-avoidance is voidable at the company's election — not void. The company may choose to affirm the transaction (especially if it is commercially advantageous) or rescind it. Rescission may be barred where: (i) the third party is an innocent purchaser for value without notice; (ii) substantial restitution has become impossible; or (iii) the company has affirmed the transaction with full knowledge. See S227(2) CA 2014 for third party protection.

questions.question: Which of the following types of conduct by a director CANNOT be ratified by a majority of shareholders, no matter how large the majority?

options.A: A director who failed to attend board meetings for six months
options.B: A director who entered a transaction that should have been approved under S238 CA 2014 (substantial non-cash assets) without obtaining approval
options.C: A director who knowingly carried on business with intent to defraud creditors — fraudulent trading under S599/S722 CA 2014
options.D: A director who exercised a power for an improper purpose but the company suffered no loss

questions.explanation: Fraudulent trading under S722/S599 CA 2014 involves a statutory criminal offence and cannot be ratified by shareholders — it is not a wrong merely against the company but involves conduct contrary to public policy and the interests of creditors and the general public. A shareholder resolution cannot remove criminal liability. This principle flows from Cook v Deeks and the broader rule that shareholders cannot ratify wrongs that affect creditors or that constitute crimes. S238 non-approval (Option B) can be ratified or affirmed in certain circumstances under S238(5).

questions.question: An injunction is sought against a director who is about to divulge commercially sensitive company information to a competitor. Which type of injunction is appropriate and what is the relevant threshold?

options.A: A mandatory injunction requiring the director to return documents — the balance of convenience test applies
options.B: A Mareva (freezing) injunction — requiring proof of an arguable case and a real risk of dissipation of assets
options.C: An interlocutory injunction restraining the disclosure — the American Cyanamid/Campus Oil threshold applies: serious question to be tried, balance of convenience and adequacy of damages as a remedy
options.D: A search order (Anton Piller order) — ordered automatically where confidential information is threatened

questions.explanation: Where a director threatens to misuse confidential information, the company may seek an interlocutory injunction. Under the Campus Oil test (derived from American Cyanamid Co v Ethicon [1975] HL, adopted in Ireland in Campus Oil v Minister for Industry [1983] IR 82), the court asks: (i) is there a serious question to be tried?; (ii) would damages be an adequate remedy?; and (iii) where does the balance of convenience lie? Given that disclosure of confidential information is often irreversible, damages are typically inadequate and the balance of convenience favours restraint.

questions.question: DecoBuild Ltd paid its managing director, Gary, a bonus of €300,000 without board or shareholder approval, in breach of his service contract terms. The company has entered liquidation. The liquidator seeks recovery. Under which statutory provision and/or equitable principle can the liquidator pursue Gary?

options.A: S599 CA 2014 — fraudulent trading, if the bonus was paid with intent to defraud creditors
options.B: S233 CA 2014 — account of profits / compensation for breach of duty; the bonus was a benefit obtained by Gary in breach of his duties and must be disgorged
options.C: S238 CA 2014 — substantial non-cash asset transaction requires prior shareholder approval
options.D: S819 CA 2014 — the liquidator may apply for Gary's restriction, but this does not result in recovery of the bonus

questions.explanation: An unauthorised payment to a director (extracted in breach of duty and without proper approval) is recoverable under S233 CA 2014 as a remedy for breach of the director's duties, and/or as a constructive trust/proprietary remedy under equity. The director must account for a benefit obtained in breach of their fiduciary duty. S238 covers transactions with value in excess of the statutory threshold but the core remedy here flows from S233 and the director's general accountability for self-dealing.

questions.question: Following removal under S146, a director launches an action against the company alleging that her removal was oppressive under S212 CA 2014. She argues that removing her constituted unfairly prejudicial conduct against her interests as a member. In what circumstances would this argument succeed?

options.A: It would always succeed as removal of a shareholder-director is per se oppressive
options.B: It would succeed only if the removal was procedurally defective (e.g., notice not given)
options.C: It may succeed where the company is a quasi-partnership, the director was brought in on a legitimate expectation of ongoing participation in management, and the removal was contrary to that expectation without adequate justification
options.D: It cannot succeed — removal under S146 is an absolute statutory right and cannot constitute oppression

questions.explanation: While S146 creates an absolute right to remove a director by ordinary resolution, removal can constitute oppression under S212 CA 2014 where the company is a quasi-partnership in which the director was brought in with a legitimate expectation of participating in management, and the removal was in breach of that expectation without adequate justification. This is drawn from Re Murph's Restaurant [1979] and Re Irish Visiting Motorists Bureau [1972]. Procedural defects (Option B) give rise to a separate remedy but are not the primary basis for a S212 claim.

questions.question: A director of an insolvent company used company funds to pay a personal debt of €50,000 while the company owed creditors €2m. The liquidator brings a claim under S233 CA 2014 and seeks to make the director personally liable. What must the liquidator establish?

options.A: That the director acted with actual dishonest intent to defraud creditors
options.B: That the director breached a duty owed to the company (in this case by misapplying company assets for personal benefit), and that the company suffered loss as a result — being the €50,000 diverted from company assets
options.C: That the company was balance-sheet insolvent at the time of the payment
options.D: That the director voted in favour of the payment at a board meeting attended by at least two other directors

questions.explanation: S233 CA 2014 is a civil remedy for breach of directors' duties. The liquidator must establish: (i) a duty owed by the director to the company; (ii) a breach of that duty; and (iii) causally resulting loss. Misapplication of company funds for personal use is a clear breach of S228(1)(a) (bona fide for the company) and constitutes a misuse of company property. The loss is the €50,000 diverted. This is a civil claim — actual dishonest intent is required only for fraudulent trading (S722) not for S233.

questions.question: Noel, having been removed as a director of RecycleOne Ltd, seeks to rely on an indemnity clause in the company's constitution that purports to protect directors from liability for 'any breach of duty whether wilful or otherwise.' Under Irish company law, to what extent is this clause effective?

options.A: The clause is fully effective — the constitution is a contract between shareholders and the company and the indemnity clause is binding
options.B: The clause may limit indemnity for negligent acts but cannot exclude liability for breach of the no-conflict duty, fraud, or dishonest conduct
options.C: Indemnity clauses in company constitutions are automatically void under the CA 2014 for any form of breach
options.D: The clause is effective for all breaches if ratified by unanimous shareholder resolution

questions.explanation: Irish company law, consistent with UK practice under the Companies Act 2006, places limits on the effectiveness of indemnity clauses: a company cannot indemnify directors against liability arising from their own fraud, dishonest conduct, or wilful breach of fiduciary duty (analogous to the English prohibition in S232 CA 2006). A clause purporting to provide blanket indemnity for 'any breach whether wilful or otherwise' will be read down to exclude fraudulent and dishonest conduct. Indemnity for negligence may be permissible subject to the constitution and any D&O insurance arrangements.

questions.question: The shareholders of Celtic Prints Ltd pass an ordinary resolution ratifying a director's breach of duty — a failure to obtain competitive quotes before awarding a contract to a company in which the director held an interest. The breach caused a €40,000 overpayment. Which of the following best describes the legal effect of the ratification?

options.A: The ratification is void — only a court can forgive a breach of fiduciary duty
options.B: If the ratification was passed with full information and without the conflicted director using his own votes to procure the majority, the ratification is effective and extinguishes the company's right to pursue the director for the €40,000
options.C: The ratification is effective only if it was unanimous — an ordinary resolution is insufficient
options.D: The ratification is effective but cannot affect the director's liability to third party creditors of the company

questions.explanation: Under Bamford v Bamford [1970], a breach of duty (other than fraud on the company or a statutory crime) can be ratified by ordinary resolution, provided: (i) members have been fully informed of all material facts; and (ii) the director has not used their own shareholding to generate the majority (which would be fraud on the minority). If these conditions are met, the ratification is effective and extinguishes the company's claim against the director for the overpayment.

questions.question: In which of the following situations is the remedy of RESCISSION of a director's conflicted transaction most likely to be UNAVAILABLE?

options.A: Where the company discovers the conflict six months after entering the contract
options.B: Where the company has continued to perform the contract for two years with full knowledge of the director's conflict, has received all the bargained-for benefits, and a third party innocent purchaser has subsequently acquired rights under the contract
options.C: Where the director is no longer a director of the company at the time the company seeks rescission
options.D: Where the transaction was approved by the board but not by the shareholders

questions.explanation: Rescission of a voidable transaction (a contract tainted by breach of the director's conflict duty) will be barred where: (i) the company affirmed the contract with full knowledge of the breach (affirmation); (ii) complete restitution is impossible (the benefits have been consumed); or (iii) an innocent third party has acquired rights for value. Option B presents all three bars simultaneously: the company affirmed (two years of performance with knowledge), restitution is impractical, and a third party has acquired rights. Once these bars arise, the company is limited to a claim in damages.
